what:
OpenFn gmail adaptor. The adaptor lets OpenFn workflows read from and write to Gmail API.

It exposes 2 operations that you can call from a workflow step, listed in the Operations tab. 12 ready-to-paste snippets are grouped as create (10) and search (2). Vendor homepage: https://developers.google.com/gmail/api.
connect:
Google supports OAuth 2.0 for API access.

The credential schema asks for access_token (required). Point the credential at the base URL: https://gmail.googleapis.com. Auth reference: https://developers.google.com/gmail/api/auth/about-auth.
when:
Reach for this adaptor when your workflow needs to read or write data in Gmail API.

8 triggers are catalogued (7 webhook, 1 polling), including New Attachment, New Email, New Email Matching Search, New Label, and more. See the Triggers tab for the full list. Google can push events to OpenFn via webhooks (docs: https://developers.google.com/gmail/api/guides/push), so reactive workflows are a good fit.
wire:
A typical wiring pattern is trigger → fetch → transform → write.

Kick the workflow off from a trigger on the Triggers tab (webhook-driven where supported, polling otherwise). Then chain operations from this adaptor's Operations tab to fetch or write data in Google. Pass values between steps through state.data — every operation reads from and writes to job state.

To move data between systems, follow this step with another adaptor (e.g. write the fetched records into a downstream FHIR, DHIS2, or database adaptor).
scale:
When running Google jobs at volume:

- Prefer Google webhooks over polling — they cut latency and rate-limit pressure.
- Paginate list and search calls explicitly; never assume a single response is the full result set.
- Make jobs idempotent by keying on stable external IDs so a replay doesn't create duplicates.
- Surface 4xx/5xx responses with the vendor request ID in your logs, and treat 429s as retries with backoff.

Adapter note: Push notifications via Cloud Pub/Sub.
best:
- Store Google credentials in OpenFn's credential store; never inline keys or tokens in job code.
- Pin @openfn/language-gmail to v2.0.14 in package.json so an upstream change doesn't break a production workflow silently.
- Keep each step small — one logical API call per operation makes failures easier to diagnose and retry.
- Add defensive checks before transforming inbound payloads — vendor responses change between versions.
- Log enough context (resource id, run id, vendor request id) so an incident can be traced end-to-end.
